coredata.dc:title: Wet-spinning fluorescent alginate fibres achieved by doping PEI modified CPDs for multiple anti-counterfeiting
coredata.dc:creator: Song, Li
Qiao, Xiaolan
Sun, Jianxin
Yi, Na
Wang, Mengyue
Zhao, Zhihui
Xie, Ruyi
Chen, Weichao
Xia, Yanzhi
coredata.dc:description: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with satisfactory excitation-dependent-emission and biocompatibility had great potential in anti-counterfeiting fibres field. However, it was difficult for CPDs to combined into the fibres due to the unstable interaction between CPDs and spinnable polymer matrix. Polyethyleneimine (PEI) was used to modify CPDs (namely PEI-CPDs) for achieving stable interactions with sodium alginate (SA) by a simple method, which including the physical interaction between the amino groups of PEI-CPDs and carboxyl groups of SA and the chain entanglement between two types of polymer chains. Then alginate fibres based on PEI-CPDs (PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres) were successfully prepared by wet-spinning for the first time with less loss of PEI-CPDs. The high mechanical strength, excellent thermal stability and good biocompatibility achieved by PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres. Furthermore, the fibres exhibited the excitation-dependent-emission property. Anti-counterfeiting of the fibres was conducted on both textile and papers, which showed higher security than the existing anti-counterfeiting fibres.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized polymer dots
Sodium alginate
Fluorescent fibre
Wet spinning
Anti-counterfeiting

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Properties of the spinning dope and characterization of PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres
Mechanical properties, humidity and toxicity test of PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by ( State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibres and Eco-Textiles Qingdao University ), No. ZKT01 . China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2012T50597 ). Key Laboratory of Science & Technology of Eco-Textile, Ministry of Education, Donghua University , China (Grant No.: 2232021G-04 ). The Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (Grant No. 21ZR1401400 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) 1H NMR spectra of CPDs and PEI-CPDs in DMSO–d6 with the same concentration, (b) XPS spectra of CPDs and PEI-CPDs, high resolution XPS of C 1 s (c), O 1 s (d), N 1 s (e), (f) FT-IR spectra of the PEI, CPDs and PEI-CPDs.

(a) Absorption spectra of CPDs and PEI-CPDs, (b) PL spectra of PEI-CPDs (Pictures of CPDs (b1) and PEI-CPDs (b2) solutions under 360 nm UV), (c) PL decay curve of these PEI-CDs, (d) Fluorescence stability of PEI-CPDs in CaCl2 solutions with different concentrations.

(a) PL emission spectra of PEI-CPDs spinning dopes at different excitation wavelengths (Pictures of PEI-CPDs spinning dopes under 360 nm UV), properties of the spinning dopes (spinning dopes of PEI-CPDs had the same concentration as the pure SA solution) (b) viscosity changes with shear rate and (c) storage (G') and loss (G") moduli as functions of frequency, (d) SEM of PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres, (e) PL spectra of PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres at different excitation wavelengths and the photograph of the PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres (f).

PL emission spectra of PEI-CPDs (a), EDA-CDs (b), CPDs (c) and CTS-CDs (d) in spinning process, (e) Photographs of PEI-CPDs, EDA-CDs, CPDs and CTS-CDs in spinning dopes, coagulation bath, fluorescent fibre and washing bath under UV lamps (365 nm).

(a) Tensile stress-strain curves of PEI-CPDs/CaALG and Ca-ALG fibres (b) TG curves of the PEI-CPDs/CaALG and Ca-ALG fibres, (c) the fluorescence response of PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres to humidity, (d) In vitro toxicological assessment of Ca-ALG fibres and PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres with varying concentrations with L-929 cells.
Anti-counterfeiting pattern of “T” on cotton fabric (a) and paper (b) under room light, 365 nm, 405 nm and 488 nm illumination.
Schematic diagram of the reaction between the CPDs and PEI, and the wet-spinning process for preparing the PEI-CPDs-loaded alginate fluorescent fibre.

head.ce:title: Wet-spinning fluorescent alginate fibres achieved by doping PEI modified CPDs for multiple anti-counterfeiting

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Textiles & Clothing, State Key Laboratory for Biofibers and Eco-textiles, Collaborative Innovation Center for Eco-textiles of Shandong Province and the Ministry of Education, Qingdao University, Qingdao 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Biofibers and Eco-textiles
Collaborative Innovation Center for Eco-textiles of Shandong Province and the Ministry of Education

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Textiles & Clothing, State Key Laboratory for Biofibers and Eco-textiles, Collaborative Innovation Center for Eco-textiles of Shandong Province and the Ministry of Education, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai 201620, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory for Modification of Chemical Fibers and Polymer Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Donghua University, Shanghai, 201620, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) with satisfactory excitation-dependent-emission and biocompatibility had great potential in anti-counterfeiting fibres field. However, it was difficult for CPDs to combined into the fibres due to the unstable interaction between CPDs and spinnable polymer matrix. Polyethyleneimine (PEI) was used to modify CPDs (namely PEI-CPDs) for achieving stable interactions with sodium alginate (SA) by a simple method, which including the physical interaction between the amino groups of PEI-CPDs and carboxyl groups of SA and the chain entanglement between two types of polymer chains. Then alginate fibres based on PEI-CPDs (PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres) were successfully prepared by wet-spinning for the first time with less loss of PEI-CPDs. The high mechanical strength, excellent thermal stability and good biocompatibility achieved by PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres. Furthermore, the fibres exhibited the excitation-dependent-emission property. Anti-counterfeiting of the fibres was conducted on both textile and papers, which showed higher security than the existing anti-counterfeiting fibres.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent fibres have attracted much attention in anti-counterfeiting due to the excellent fluorescent stability, satisfactory concealment and large-scale industrial production (Le et al., 2021; Zhang et al., 2021). Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) owing to excitation-dependent-emission and photochemical stability was a candidate for fabricating fluorescent anti-counterfeit fibres (Bayati, Dai, Zambrana, Rees, & Fidalgo de Cortalezzi, 2018; Katzen et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022). However, the fluorescence stability of CPDs in composite fibres was difficult to realize. Therefore, it was crucial to fabricate CPDs based anti-counterfeiting fibres with stable fluorescence.

Electrospinning was usually used to fabricate fluorescent fibres containing CPDs (De Oliveira et al., 2020). Nevertheless, electrospun fibres in practical application was limited due to their unsatisfactory mechanical properties and small-scale production. Compared with electrospinning, wet-spinning was more convenient to achieve large-scale and continuous production (Kim, Kwon, & Na, 2019; Subjalearndee et al., 2022). However, low composite fastness and quenching of CPDs in spinning process made it difficult to produce fluorescent fibres by wet spinning. Therefore, the surface modification of CPDs was necessary. Polymers was an ideal surface modification reagent, which could provide mechanical support, chemical resistance and good fluorescence stability for CPDs (Du, Wang, Wu, & Chen, 2021; Long et al., 2018). Polyethyleneimine (PEI) had been used to modify the surface of CPDs because the protective layer could be formed, which improved the fluorescence stability and intensity (Hu et al., 2019; Yao, Niu, Lee, Li, & Li, 2019). For example, a facile strategy was reported to realize red emission of CPDs in aqueous solution by surface modification with PEI via microwave heating method (Wang et al., 2022). Nitrogen-doped CPDs from poly(ethyleneimine) prepared by one-step hydrothermal method were used as fluorescent probe and possessed good biocompatibility and high photostability (Liu et al., 2020a). All in all, the above PEI-CPDs need to be modified by carbonization at high temperature, resulting in increased economic and time costs. Room temperature modification could preserve long carbon chains and provide physical cross-links between PEI-CPDs and fibre matrix. Up to date, PEI modified CPDs at room temperature had not been reported. Therefore, it was significant to prepare CPDs based fluorescent anti-counterfeiting fibres by PEI modification at room temperature.

Sodium alginate (SA) as a natural polysaccharide polymer had the advantage of biodegradability, biocompatibility, non-toxicity, flame retardancy and low cost (Salehi et al., 2019; Yang, Guo, Zhang, & Gong, 2017). It could be processed to fibres by wet-spinning. Moreover, a large amount of carboxyl groups in SA had a strong physical interaction with amino of PEI-CPDs, which could enhance the fastness of CPDs in fibres. This interaction could promote the uniform dispersion of PEI-CPDs in fibres and enabled the preparation of fluorescent fibres with stable fluorescence properties.

Based on these considerations, we hypothesize that multiple-emitting fluorescent fibres could be prepared by incorporating PEI-CPDs into calcium alginate fibres by wet-spinning. Here, PEI-CPDs were synthesized at room temperature and the chemical composition and optical properties were investigated. Then, PEI-CPDs doped calcium alginate fibres (namely PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres) were fabricated and the properties of the fibres were discussed. The fastness of PEI-CPDs in the fibres was discussed and compared with several other CPDs. The fibres were further applicated onto the cotton textile and papers to examine the anti-counterfeiting properties. Our work provides a positive guiding significance for the preparation of CPDs-based anti-counterfeiting fluorescent fibres by wet spinning.

Sodium alginate (SA, chemical pure, Mw = 220 kDa, MWD = 1.5 and M/G = 1.05) was purchased from Qingdao Ming Yue seaweed Group Co., Ltd. Polyethyleneimine (PEI, M.W. 10,000) was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. CaCl2, HCl and glutaraldehyde were bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Distilled water was used for all the experiments.

SA aqueous solutions (50 mL) with the concentration of 2 wt% was prepared. Subsequently, 800 μL of glutaraldehyde and 1 mL of 0.1 M HCl were added and mixed in the above solution. The mixture was stirred for 4 h, then evenly divided and transferred into two poly(tetrafluoroethylene)-lined autoclaves (50 mL) and heated at 180 °C for 6 h (Sun, Yu, Jiang, Zhao, & Xia, 2020). The obtained brown solution was filtered through a 0.45 μm nylon filter membrane (Millipore) to remove the carbonized precipitate. Resulted CPDs solution was obtained by dialysis with water in a dialysis bag (1000 D) for 3 days. Solid CPDs powders were obtained via freeze-drying.

CPDs and PEI aqueous solution with the concentrations of 2 wt% and 6 wt% were prepared respectively and then mixed with a ratio of 1:3. The mixture was stirred for 6 h at room temperature until the color was uniform and clear. The mixture was finally dialyzed with the dialysis bag of 10 kDa for 24 h and the dialysate was changed every 3 h. PEI-CPDs in the bag was collected by concentration and lyophilization.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization of the PEI-CPDs was recorded on a HITACHI H-7600. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements was performed with ESCALAB 250 Xi XPS system off Thermo Scientific (USA). Nuclear magnetic resonance spectra (1H NMR) were measured with a Bruker AV-400 (Germany) using DSMO-d6 as a solvent. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) was recorded with a Nicolet 6700 Spectrometer (USA). Absorption spectra were recorded were measured by a HITACHI U-3900H spectrophotometer (Japan). Steady-state and time-resolved PL characterization of the PEI-CPDs was performed on an Edinburgh FS5 instrument (Britain). Quantum yield (QY%) was measured according to the method in the literature (Y. Wang et al., 2018).

300 mL of SA aqueous solution (5 wt%) and 20 mL of PEI-CPDs (0.5 wt%) were mixed and stirred for 2–3 h at room temperature to ensure the PEI-CPDs was evenly dispersed in the SA solution. Then the mixture was extruded quantitatively by a metering pump through a spinneret into a coagulation bath of 5.0 wt% CaCl2 aqueous solution and finally collected on a roller. The obtained fibres were washed thoroughly with water to remove the excessive CaCl2 attached to the fibres' surface. Then the fibres were dried by lyophilization (Scheme 1 ).

To measure the fastness of PEI-CPDs in the CaALG fibres, the coagulation bath and washing bath were collected and the PL properties of the baths were measured. Meanwhile, same procedures were conducted using three other kinds of CPDs with different surface properties to estimate the advancement of PEI modification to the fastness of CPDs and CaALG fibres.

Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) was carried with QUANTA FEG 250, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Massachusetts, MA (USA). Rheological analysis was performed with a rotational rheometer (PhysicaMCR301, Anton Paar, Graz, Austria). Fluorescence spectra refer to previous equipments. Thermogravimetric (TG) curves were generated in a STA 6000 analyzer (PerkinElmer Instruments; Shelton, USA) by a heating rate of 10 °C/min with nitrogen purging. The tensile test was obtained by an INSTRON-5965 universal testing machine (Instron, US, tensile speed: 5 mm/min).

Fluorescent papers were prepared using the Rapid Kothen method.(Sehaqui, Liu, Zhou, & Berglund, 2010; Tan et al., 2020) First, 500 g of PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres were immersed in 5 L of water for 6 h. Then the fibres were beaten to pulp in 30 L of water with a Varley beater. 10 g of pulp was then dispersed in 2.5 L of water with a standard fibre dissociator at 1000 rpm for 20 min. Finally, the homogeneous slurry was transferred to a sheet former for papermaking. The obtained paper was dried at 70 °C for 1 h.

The cell cytotoxicity of PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres were tested by the MTT assay with L-929 fibroblasts. The procedure was similar with our previous work except for the gradient concentrations of the fibres in each well were 0, 16, 32, 61, 125, 250 μg/mL, respectively (Sun et al., 2020). All samples were performed five times to obtain an average value.

The morphologies of CPDs and PEI-CPDs were characterized by TEM as shown in Fig. S1. Compared with CPDs (2.72 nm), PEI-CPDs showed the larger average diameter of 12.49 nm due to the wrapping of PEI chains. Clear crystal structure with the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm was found in the PEI-CPDs, which belonged to the (100) plane of graphite carbon. Since the lattice was not successfully observed in CPDs, it was reasonable to believe the modification of PEI made the crystal structure of the CPDs more stable.

1H NMR was conducted to explore the chemical composition of PEI-CPDs. The vanished proton signals in 13.5–11.0 ppm for PEI-CPDs belonging to carboxyl groups indicated that nearly all the carboxyl groups on CPDs reacted with amino groups of PEI as shown in Fig. 1(a) (Wang, Wang, et al., 2022). These proved that CPDs was successfully modified by PEI. XPS analysis was used to verify the chemical structural evolutions from CPDs to PEI-CPDs. XPS analysis (Fig. 1(b)) revealed the new appearance of N element for PEI-CPDs. High-resolution C 1 s and O 1 s spectra were shown in Fig. 1(c-d). By contrast, the -COOH (289 eV) disappeared and the C-OH/C-O-C (532.5 eV) was replaced by O-C=O (534.7 eV) after PEI modification, which indicated that the amidation reaction between carboxyl groups and the amino groups (Wang et al., 2022; Yu et al., 2021). The -C=N peak located at 398.9 eV attributed to N 1 s (Fig. 1(e)) (Qiao, Yang, Han, Zhang, & Zhu, 2021; Tan et al., 2016), which indicated that Schiff base reaction may be occurred between the CPDs-displayed aldehydes and the amine residues in PEI (Bhattacharya et al., 2019). These verified that the PEI molecules were modified on the surface of CPDs, which were further confirmed by FT-IR analysis. As shown in Fig. 1(f), the generating CN bonds at 1654 cm−1 and the vanishing -CHO bonds at 1700 cm−1 also verified the occurrence of Schiff base reaction (Clapp et al., 2004; Liu et al., 2020b). The N-H stretching vibration peak and bending vibration peak appeared at 3363 cm−1 and 1569 cm−1 (Clapp et al., 2004; Liu et al., 2020a; Vallan et al., 2018), respectively. The results indicated the formation of amide bond between the amino group of PEI and the carboxyl group of CPDs. These results were consistent with XPS analysis. Zeta potential analysis (Fig. S2(a)) further demonstrated the surface PEI modification by the reversed surface charges from negativity for CPDs to positivity for PEI-CPDs. Moreover, compared with CPDs (3.06 nm), PEI-CPDs showed the larger particle diameter of 13.29 nm due to the wrapping of PEI chains (Fig. S2(b)). The result was consistent with TEM. In summary, based on the 1H NMR, XPS FT-IR data, Zeta potential and particle diameter, the surface of the CPDs was successfully modified with PEI.

The UV–Vis absorption spectra of CPDs and PEI-CPDs were shown in Fig. 2(a). After PEI modification, the absorption spectra showed a red-shift compared with CPDs. The phenomenon was attributed to the n-π* transition of conjugated CO indicating the occurrence of amidation reaction (Jiang, Wang, Gao, Cai, & Lin, 2018; Lai et al., 2020). Moreover, the fluorescence emission color and intensity also changed upon modification. As shown in the photo of Fig. 2(b), PEI-CPDs aqueous solution exhibited red-shifted cyan color which looked more intense than the blue emission of CPDs. The QY% value confirmed that it enhanced from 11 % (CPDs) to 23 % (PEI-CPDs) in aqueous solutions. Moreover, the QY% of PEI-CPDs in ethanol was 35 %. These changes were due to the “surface passivation” by amino groups, which was a common and peculiar phenomenon of CPDs (Y. Liu et al., 2019).

The excitation-dependent-emission property was the precious characteristic of CPDs to realize multi-colored anti-counterfeiting. PEI-CPDs was verified to possess this property. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the emission of PEI-CPDs shifted from 460 nm to 550 nm as the excitation light changed from 300 nm to 400 nm, which was attributed to existence of multiple photoluminescent centers of CPDs (Wang et al., 2021; Xu et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2019). Moreover, PEI-CPDs had two lifetimes of 5.54 ns and 1.02 ns, respectively, indicating the existence of two PL centers (Fig. 2(c)). The average PL lifetime was calculated to be 3.90 ns. Such short lifetimes suggested the radiative recombination nature of excitations. The PL intensity of the PEI-CPDs was then measured periodically to explore its PL stability. As shown in Fig. S3, the PL intensity of the PEI-CPDs maintained 63.9 % of its initial value after 5 months' storage, indicating its good PL stability. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CPDs in CaCl2 solutions with different concentrations was tested (Fig. 2(d)), which would be used as the coagulation bath of the wet-spinning. It was found that CaCl2 had few effects on its fluorescence intensity, which provided a possibility for PEI-CPDs in the subsequent wet spinning process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Properties of the spinning dope and characterization of PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 3(a), the spinning dope remained excitation-dependent-emission characteristic of PEI-CPDs. Rheological measurements explored the effect of PEI-CPDs on the chain conformation and hydrodynamic behavior of SA solution. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the viscosity of spinning dope increased with the addition of PEI-CPDs, indicating the interactions between PEI-CPDs and SA. The interactions were also confirmed by dynamic viscoelasticity data. As shown in Fig. 3(c), G" was larger than the G' in the low frequency region and became lower than G' in the high frequency region, which meant that the solutions gradually changed from a viscous fluid to elastomers with increasing shearing frequency. Moreover, the crossover point moved to lower frequency with the addition PEI-CPDs. This phenomenon might benefit from the fact that the interaction between PEI-CPDs and SA increased the chain entanglement (Nie et al., 2008). These results provide evidence for the fastness between PEI-CPDs and SA in spinning process.

Compared with CaALG fibres (11.2 μm in Fig. S4), PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres showed the larger average diameter of 20.9 μm (Fig. 3(d)). The result might because that the interaction between alginate and PEI-CPDs increased chain entanglement and the viscosity of the spinning dope, which resulted in a larger fibre diameter. As shown in Fig. 3(e), the emission peak position showed a red-shift from 460 nm (cyan) to 550 nm (green) as the exciation wavelengths changed from 310 nm to 410 nm. Therefore, the fibres had an excitation-dependent-emission property. The PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres had preeminent multi-color fluorescence feature under different wavelengths of light as shown in Fig. 3(f). Meanwhile, the CaALG fibres had no fluorescence feature under different wavelengths of light (Fig. S5).

Moreover, the PL stability of the PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres was investigated by measuring the PL intensities of the fibres stored in dark at different time period. The result (Fig. S6(a)) showed that 64.5 % of its initial intensity was maintained after 5 months' storage. In addition, to test the photostability of the PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres, it was placed in a laboratory without any protection (25 °C, humidity of 40 %). Both sunlight and room light could shine on the fibres. The PL intensity of the fibres was measured periodically. The results showed that the PL intensity of the anti-counterfeiting fibres could maintain 84.8 % of its initial value after 72 h (Fig. S6(b)), which provided a good basis for the application of anti-counterfeiting.

To further investigated the effect of PEI modification preparing CPDs-based fluorescent fibres by wet spinning. Three kinds CDs and CPDs with different surface properties were also used to prepare fibres, including CPDs (negative charged), EDA-CDs (electrically neutral) (Zhu et al., 2013) and CTS-CDs (positive charged) (Chowdhury, Gogoi, & Majumdar, 2012). As shown in Fig. 4 , all four spinning dopes exhibited good fluorescence. The coagulation baths of EDA-CDs and CPDs have strong emission and the fibres containing CTS-CDs and CPDs had none obvious fluorescence. It could be speculated that the fluorescence of CTS-CDs quenched in the coagulation bath, and as a result none fluorescence was observed after the wet-spinning process. The fluorescence intensity of EDA-CDs in the fibres was much lower than those in the spinning dope and coagulation bath, and weak fluorescence could only be observed in the washing bath of EDA-CDs. The phenomenon suggested that EDA-CDs and CPDs leaked in spinning process because the interaction between CDs/CPDs and water was stronger than alginate fibres. For PEI-CPDs, PEI could form a protective layer on the surface of CPDs to prevent quenching. Meanwhile, the amines on the surface of PEI-CPDs could react with carboxyl groups on the alginate to form hydrogen bonds and improve the chain entanglement. Therefore, PEI modification was a promising modified method for the preparation of CPDs-based fluorescent fibres by wet-spinning.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical properties, humidity and toxicity test of PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres
ce:section.ce:para: Mechanical properties of the fluorescent fibres were important. Stress-strain curves were presented in Fig. 5(a). The breaking strength increased from 1.86 cN/dtex to 2.14 cN/dtex and the breaking also increased from 37.9 % to 49.6 % after addition PEI-CPDs, which was benefit from the strong interaction between the SA and PEI-CPDs.

Furthermore, XRD 2-D was conducted to explain the strength change. 2D-XRD were performed on Smart Lab DX2900 with λ = 1.5418 Å under transmission mode. The 2D and 1D-XRD patterns were shown in Fig. S7. The 1D-XRD curves were integrated from the 2D-XRD patterns. For the CaALG fibres, two weak diffraction rings appeared at q = 9.61 nm−1 and q = 14.75 nm−1 (Fig. S7(a)), corresponding to the diffraction peaks of 13.52° and 20.84°, respectively (Fig. S7(c)). The broad diffraction rings indicated its random orientation and amorphous behavior. For the PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres, a new narrow diffraction ring appeared at q = 20.67 nm−1(2θ = 29.32°, Fig.S7(d)), which was indexed to the crystalline peak of PEI-CPDs. More important, the diffraction arc at q = 9.61 nm−1 only appeared in q z-axis (marked in the red circle of Fig.S7(b)). The results revealed that the fibres possessed preferential orientation along the q z direction, which were benefit from the strong interaction between the SA and PEI-CPDs and responsible for the increased strength of PEI-CPD/CaALG fibres.

TG measurement used for studying thermal stability of composite fibres. As shown in Fig. 5(b), both fibres went through a similar weight lose process. The degradations below 100 °C almost coincided, corresponding to the water loss. The weight losses in the range of 100–220 °C and 220–310 °C corresponded to the destruction of glycosidic and the intermolecular hydrogen bonds, respectively (Xiao, Liu, Lu, & Na, 2001). The degradation rates of PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres were slower than CaALG fibres during the two stages, which provided evidence for the strong interaction between PEI-CPDs and CaALG fibres. Therefore, the PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres had good thermal stability.

As shown in Fig. 5(c), when the humidity reached 90 %, the fluorescence intensity of PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres remained 82.3 % of its initial value. The emission wavelength did not change at all (Fig. S8). The results showed that the humidity has little effect on the fluorescence stability of the fibres and provided the possibility for the application of the fibres in the field of anti-counterfeiting.

In order to ensure the safety of PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres in its daily applications, the in vitro toxicity of PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres was determined by the MTT cell survival test. As shown in Fig. 5(d) that the cell viability was higher than 95 % at all concentrations, indicating that PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres had enough safety for daily usages.

The fibres were used for anti-counterfeiting because of their photostability, excitation-dependence, biosafety and large-scale production. The preliminary usages of PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres in the anti-counterfeiting of textile and papers were then carried out. As shown in Fig. 6(a), a simple knitted pattern “T” in a cotton textile was prepared via needlework. The knitted patterns which was invisible under room light, could be found and displayed different color under different wavelength. Cyan, green and red patterns could be achieved under 365 nm, 405 nm and 488 nm illumination, respectively. Multiple anti-counterfeiting properties obtained in PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres. Moreover, the fibres were also used to prepare the paper by the Rapid-Köthen method. As shown in Fig. 6(b), paper also had multiple anti-counterfeiting properties, which indicated the fluorescence of fibres were kept during the process. Obviously, the multi-color emission property of the PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres would provide higher-level security than monochromatic fibres in the flexible anti-counterfeiting field. Meanwhile, textiles were fabricated by using the PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres (Fig. S9), which may open possibilities to use CPDs-based multiple anti-counterfeiting fluorescent fibres for potential flexible anti-counterfeiting applications.

In conclusion, CPDs-based florescent fibres with excitation-dependent-emission properties were successfully fabricated using wet-spinning for the first time by PEI modification. The results showed PEI modification improved the fastness between CPDs and SA, and the prepared PEI-CPDs had excitation-dependent-emission properties and exhibited relatively high PLQY of 23 % in aqueous solution. The PEI-CPDs/CaALG fibres had satisfactory excitation-dependent-emission, mechanical properties (up to 2.14 cN/dtex), thermal stability and cyto-compatibility. The knitted patterns and papers prepared by fibres also showed multi-color fluorescence performance and higher anti-counterfeiting level. It provided a reliable method for the large-scale preparation of CPDs-based multiple anti-counterfeiting fluorescent fibre via wet spinning, and also facilitated the design of novel wearable optical sensors for anti-counterfeiting.

Li Song: Writing – original draft, Data curation, Visualization, Formal analysis. Xiaolan Qiao: Methodology, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing. Jianxin Sun: Investigation, Data curation, Validation, Methodology. Na Yi: Data curation, Methodology. Mengyue Wang: Software, Resources, Methodology. Zhihui Zhao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Ruyi Xie: Software, Resources, Investigation. Weichao Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Supervision. Yanzhi Xia: Conceptualization, Investigation, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by (State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibres and Eco-Textiles Qingdao University), No. ZKT01. China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2012T50597). Key Laboratory of Science & Technology of Eco-Textile, Ministry of Education, Donghua University, China (Grant No.: 2232021G-04). The Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (Grant No. 21ZR1401400).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbpol.2022.120500.
